1.title: RepoForge: Training a SOTA Fast-thinking SWE Agent with an End-to-End Data Curation Pipeline Synergizing SFT and RL at Scale

Challenge 2: Inefficient and Sequential Reward Evaluation Pipelines
Challenge 3: Limited Availability of High-Quality SWE Training Data
Challenge 4: High Cost and Subjectivity of Manual Data Labelling
Challenge 5: Agentic Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning Pipeline Bottlenecks
Challenge 1 Solution – Storage‑Efficient Lineage Image Reuse & Runtime Environment De-bloating to Reduce Disk Usage
Challenge 2 Solution – RepoForge Harness \- A Ray-Powered Distributed Efficient Evaluation Engine

2.title: More blog posts are coming soon!
2.description: We will keep posting more blog posts and articles in the future.

3.title: Write TSX Articles
3.description: This is an example of a TSX documentation page with interactive features.

4.title: SPICE: An Automated SWE-Bench Labeling Pipeline for Issue Clarity, Test Coverage, and Effort Estimation
4.section: Research / SPICE: An Automated SWE-Bench Labeling Pipeline for Issue Clarity, Test Coverage, and Effort Estimation
4.description: We introduce SPICE, a scalable, automated pipeline for labeling SWE-bench-style datasets with annotations for issue clarity, test coverage, and effort estimation. SPICE combines context-aware code navigation, rationale-driven prompting, and multi-pass consensus to produce labels that closely approximate expert annotations. Our evaluation shows that SPICE achieves high inter-annotator agreement and can be used to improve the quality of software engineering datasets.

5.title: Context-Aware CodeLLM Eviction for AI-assisted Coding
5.section: Research / Context-Aware CodeLLM Eviction for AI-assisted Coding
5.description: We present a context-aware eviction strategy for CodeLLMs in AI-assisted coding environments. Our approach considers the contextual relevance and usage patterns of code completions to optimize memory utilization and response time. The method demonstrates improved performance in resource-constrained environments while maintaining code suggestion quality.

6.title: Adaptive Request Scheduling for CodeLLM Serving with SLA Guarantees
6.section: Research / Adaptive Request Scheduling for CodeLLM Serving with SLA Guarantees
6.description: We present an adaptive request scheduling system for serving Code Large Language Models (CodeLLMs) with Service Level Agreement (SLA) guarantees. Our approach dynamically adjusts scheduling policies based on request characteristics and system load to optimize both throughput and latency while maintaining SLA compliance. Experimental results show significant improvements in serving efficiency compared to static scheduling approaches.

7.title: Towards Conversational Development Environments: Using Theory-of-Mind and Multi-Agent Architectures for Requirements Refinement
7.section: Research / Towards Conversational Development Environments: Using Theory-of-Mind and Multi-Agent Architectures for Requirements Refinement
7.description: Foundation Models (FMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in various natural language tasks. However, their ability to accurately capture stakeholder requirements remains a significant challenge for using FMs for software development. This paper introduces a novel approach that leverages an FM-powered multi-agent system called AlignMind to address this issue. By having a cognitive architecture that enhances FMs with Theory-of-Mind capabilities, our approach considers the mental states and perspectives of software makers. This allows our solution to iteratively clarify the beliefs, desires, and intentions of stakeholders, translating these into a set of refined requirements and a corresponding actionable natural language workflow in the often-overlooked requirements refinement phase of software engineering, which is crucial after initial elicitation.

8.title: Model Performance-Guided Evaluation Data Selection for Effective Prompt Optimization
8.section: Research / Model Performance-Guided Evaluation Data Selection for Effective Prompt Optimization
8.description: Optimizing Large Language Model (LLM) performance requires well-crafted prompts, but manual prompt engineering is labor-intensive and often ineffective. Automated prompt optimization techniques address this challenge but the majority of them rely on randomly selected evaluation subsets, which fail to represent the full dataset, leading to unreliable evaluations and suboptimal prompts. Existing coreset selection methods, designed for LLM benchmarking, are unsuitable for prompt optimization due to challenges in clustering similar samples, high data collection costs, and the unavailability of performance data for new or private datasets.

9.title: The Hitchhikers Guide to Production-ready Trustworthy Foundation Model powered Software (FMware)
9.section: Research / The Hitchhikers Guide to Production-ready Trustworthy Foundation Model powered Software (FMware)
9.description: Foundation Models (FMs) such as Large Language Models (LLMs) are reshaping the software industry by enabling FMware, systems that integrate these FMs as core components. In this KDD 2025 tutorial, we present a comprehensive exploration of FMware that combines a curated catalogue of challenges with real-world production concerns. We first discuss the state of research and practice in building FMware. We further examine the difficulties in selecting suitable models, aligning high-quality domain-specific data, engineering robust prompts, and orchestrating autonomous agents.

10.title: SLA-Awareness for AI-assisted coding
10.description: We introduce SLA-aware mechanisms for AI-assisted coding systems that ensure service level agreement compliance while providing code suggestions. Our approach balances code quality, response time, and system reliability to meet specified SLA requirements. The system demonstrates consistent performance across various coding scenarios and workloads.

11.title: MetaSel: A Test Selection Approach for Fine-tuned DNN Models
11.section: Research / MetaSel: A Test Selection Approach for Fine-tuned DNN Models
11.description: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) face challenges during deployment due to data distribution shifts. Fine-tuning adapts pre-trained models to new contexts requiring smaller labeled sets. However, testing fine-tuned models under constrained labeling budgets remains a critical challenge. This paper introduces MetaSel, a new approach, tailored for fine-tuned DNN models, to select tests from unlabeled inputs. MetaSel assumes that fine-tuned and pre-trained models share related data distributions and exhibit similar behaviors for many inputs.

12.title: Simclone: detecting tabular data clones using value similarity
12.section: Research / Simclone: detecting tabular data clones using value similarity
12.description: Data clones are defined as multiple copies of the same data among datasets. The presence of data clones between datasets can cause issues such as difficulties in managing data assets and data license violations when using datasets with clones to build AI software. However, detecting data clones is not trivial. The majority of the prior studies in this area rely on structural information to detect data clones (e.g., font size, column header). However, tabular datasets used to build AI software are typically stored without any structural information.

13.title: A Framework and Taxonomy for Characterizing the Applicability of Software Architecture Recovery Approaches: A Tertiary‐Mapping Study
13.section: Research / A Framework and Taxonomy for Characterizing the Applicability of Software Architecture Recovery Approaches: A Tertiary‐Mapping Study
13.description: Software architecture assists developers in addressing non-functional requirements and in maintaining, debugging, and upgrading their software systems. Consequently, consistency between the designed architecture and the implemented software system itself is important; without this consistency the non-functional requirements targeted may not be addressed and architectural documentation may mis-direct maintenance efforts that target the associated code-base.

14.title: Engineering AI Judge Systems
14.description: AI judge systems are designed to automatically evaluate Foundation Model-powered software (i.e., FMware). Due to the intrinsic dynamic and stochastic nature of FMware, the development of AI judge systems requires a unique engineering life cycle and presents new challenges. In this paper, we discuss the challenges based on our industrial experiences in developing AI judge systems for FMware. These challenges lead to substantial time consumption, cost and inaccurate judgments.

15.title: Real-time Adapting Routing (RAR): Improving Efficiency Through Continuous Learning in Software Powered by Layered Foundation Models
15.section: Research / Real-time Adapting Routing (RAR): Improving Efficiency Through Continuous Learning in Software Powered by Layered Foundation Models
15.description: To balance the quality and inference cost of a Foundation Model (FM, such as large language models (LLMs)) powered software, people often opt to train a routing model that routes requests to FMs with different sizes and capabilities. Existing routing models rely on learning the optimal routing decision from carefully curated data, require complex computations to be updated, and do not consider the potential evolution of weaker FMs.

16.title: Software Performance Engineering for Foundation Model-Powered Software (FMware)
16.section: Research / Software Performance Engineering for Foundation Model-Powered Software (FMware)
16.description: We present a comprehensive approach to software performance engineering specifically designed for Foundation Model-powered software (FMware). Our methodology addresses the unique performance challenges posed by integrating foundation models into software systems, including latency optimization, resource management, and scalability considerations. The framework provides practical guidelines for developing high-performance FMware applications.

17.title: Watson: A Cognitive Observability Framework for the Reasoning of LLM-Powered Agents
17.section: Research / Watson: A Cognitive Observability Framework for the Reasoning of LLM-Powered Agents
17.description: As foundation models (FMs) play an increasingly prominent role in complex software systems, such as agentic software, they introduce significant observability and debuggability challenges. Although recent Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) generate their thought processes as part of the output, in many scenarios fast-thinking Large Language Models (LLMs) are still preferred due to latency constraints. LLM-powered agents operate autonomously with opaque implicit reasoning, making it difficult to debug their unexpected behaviors or errors.

18.title: From Cool Demos to Production-Ready FMware: Core Challenges and a Technology Roadmap
18.section: Research / From Cool Demos to Production-Ready FMware: Core Challenges and a Technology Roadmap
18.description: The rapid expansion of foundation models (FMs), such as large language models (LLMs), has given rise to FMware--software systems that integrate FMs as core components. While building demonstration-level FMware is relatively straightforward, transitioning to production-ready systems presents numerous challenges, including reliability, high implementation costs, scalability, and compliance with privacy regulations.

19.title: Teasma: A practical methodology for test adequacy assessment of deep neural networks
19.section: Research / Teasma: A practical methodology for test adequacy assessment of deep neural networks
19.description: Successful deployment of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), particularly in safety-critical systems, requires their validation with an adequate test set to ensure a sufficient degree of confidence in test outcomes. Although well-established test adequacy assessment techniques from traditional software, such as mutation analysis and coverage criteria, have been adapted to DNNs in recent years, we still need to investigate their application within a comprehensive methodology for accurately predicting the fault detection ability of test sets and thus assessing their adequacy.

20.title: Promptexp: Multi-granularity prompt explanation of large language models
20.section: Research / Promptexp: Multi-granularity prompt explanation of large language models
20.description: Large Language Models excel in tasks like natural language understanding and text generation. Prompt engineering plays a critical role in leveraging LLM effectively. However, LLMs black-box nature hinders its interpretability and effective prompting engineering. A wide range of model explanation approaches have been developed for deep learning models, However, these local explanations are designed for single-output tasks like classification and regression,and cannot be directly applied to LLMs, which generate sequences of tokens.

21.title: Towards AI-Native Software Engineering (SE 3.0): A Vision and a Challenge Roadmap
21.section: Research / Towards AI-Native Software Engineering (SE 3.0): A Vision and a Challenge Roadmap
21.description: The rise of AI-assisted software engineering (SE 2.0), powered by Foundation Models (FMs) and FM-powered copilots, has shown promise in improving developer productivity. However, it has also exposed inherent limitations, such as cognitive overload on developers and inefficiencies. We propose a shift towards Software Engineering 3.0 (SE 3.0), an AI-native approach characterized by intent-first, conversation-oriented development between human developers and AI teammates.

22.title: Data quality antipatterns for software analytics
22.section: Research / Data quality antipatterns for software analytics
22.description: Background: Data quality is vital in software analytics, particularly for machine learning (ML) applications like software defect prediction (SDP). Despite the widespread use of ML in software engineering, the effect of data quality antipatterns on these models remains underexplored. Objective: This study develops a taxonomy of ML-specific data quality antipatterns and assesses their impact on software analytics models' performance and interpretation.

23.title: A Tutorial on Software Engineering for FMware
23.section: Research / A Tutorial on Software Engineering for FMware
23.description: Foundation Models (FMs) like GPT-4 have given rise to FMware, FM-powered applications representing a new generation of software that is developed with new roles, assets, and paradigms. FMware has been widely adopted in both software engineering (SE) research (e.g., test generation) and industrial products (e.g., GitHub copilot), despite the numerous challenges introduced by the stochastic nature of FMs.

24.title: Rethinking software engineering in the era of foundation models: A curated catalogue of challenges in the development of trustworthy fmware
24.section: Research / Rethinking software engineering in the era of foundation models: A curated catalogue of challenges in the development of trustworthy fmware
24.description: Foundation models (FMs), such as Large Language Models (LLMs), have revolutionized software development by enabling new use cases and business models. We refer to software built using FMs as FMware. The unique properties of FMware (e.g., prompts, agents and the need for orchestration), coupled with the intrinsic limitations of FMs (e.g., hallucination) lead to a completely new set of software engineering challenges.

25.title: Keeping deep learning models in check: A history-based approach to mitigate overfitting
25.section: Research / Keeping deep learning models in check: A history-based approach to mitigate overfitting
25.description: In software engineering, deep learning models are increasingly deployed for critical tasks such as bug detection and code review. However, overfitting remains a challenge that affects the quality, reliability, and trustworthiness of software systems that utilize deep learning models. Overfitting can be (1) prevented (e.g., using dropout or early stopping) or (2) detected in a trained model (e.g., using correlation-based approaches).

26.title: A Framework for Real-time Safeguarding the Text Generation of Large Language Model
26.section: Research / A Framework for Real-time Safeguarding the Text Generation of Large Language Model
26.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly advanced natural language processing (NLP) tasks but also pose ethical and societal risks due to their propensity to generate harmful content. Existing methods have limitations, including the need for training specific control models and proactive intervention during text generation, that lead to quality degradation and increased computational overhead.

27.title: Rethinking Software Engineering in the Foundation Model Era: From Task-Driven AI Copilots to Goal-Driven AI Pair Programmers
27.section: Research / Rethinking Software Engineering in the Foundation Model Era: From Task-Driven AI Copilots to Goal-Driven AI Pair Programmers
27.description: The advent of Foundation Models (FMs) and AI-powered copilots has transformed the landscape of software development, offering unprecedented code completion capabilities and enhancing developer productivity. However, the current task-driven nature of these copilots falls short in addressing the broader goals and complexities inherent in software engineering (SE). In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift towards goal-driven AI-powered pair programmers that collaborate with human developers in a more holistic and context-aware manner.

28.title: Technical Brief on Software Engineering for FMware
28.section: Research / Technical Brief on Software Engineering for FMware
28.description: Foundation Models (FM) like GPT-4 have given rise to FMware, FM-powered applications, which represent a new generation of software that is developed with new roles, assets, and paradigms. FMware has been widely adopted in both software engineering (SE) research (e.g., test generation) and industrial products (e.g., GitHub copilot), despite the numerous challenges introduced by the stochastic nature of FMs.

29.title: An Empirical Comparison on the Results of Different Clone Detection Setups for C-based Projects
29.section: Research / An Empirical Comparison on the Results of Different Clone Detection Setups for C-based Projects
29.description: Code clone detection is a fundamental technique in software engineering that helps identify similar code fragments within or across software projects. Various clone detection tools and configurations have been proposed, but their effectiveness can vary significantly depending on the setup and the characteristics of the target projects. This study presents an empirical comparison of different clone detection setups specifically for C-based projects.

30.title: Towards training reproducible deep learning models
30.section: Research / Towards training reproducible deep learning models
30.description: Reproducibility is an increasing concern in Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly in the area of Deep Learning (DL). Being able to reproduce DL models is crucial for AI-based systems, as it is closely tied to various tasks like training, testing, debugging, and auditing. However, DL models are challenging to be reproduced due to issues like randomness in the software (e.g., DL algorithms) and non-determinism in the hardware (e.g., GPU).

31.title: Towards build verifiability for java-based systems
31.section: Research / Towards build verifiability for java-based systems
31.description: We propose an approach for achieving build verifiability in Java-based software systems. Our method enables verification that compiled artifacts match their source code and build specifications, enhancing security and trust in software supply chains. The approach addresses challenges specific to Java ecosystems including dependency management and build tool integration.

32.title: Systems, methods, and non-transitory computer-readable storage devices for detecting and analyzing data clones in tabular datasets
32.section: Research / Systems, methods, and non-transitory computer-readable storage devices for detecting and analyzing data clones in tabular datasets
32.description: A computerized method for detecting and analyzing data clones in one or more dataset pairs has the steps of: obtaining one or more similarity matrices and one or more sets of readout values of the one or more similarity matrices from the dataset pairs using a data-clone detection method, each set of readout values corresponding to a similarity matrix; obtaining one or more importance values for the one or more similarity matrices by processing the one or more sets of readout values using an interpretation method.

33.title: Systems, methods, and non-transitory computer-readable storage devices for training deep learning and neural network models using overfitting detection and prevention
33.section: Research / Systems, methods, and non-transitory computer-readable storage devices for training deep learning and neural network models using overfitting detection and prevention
33.description: Systems and methods for training deep learning and neural network models with automated overfitting detection and prevention capabilities. The approach uses training history analysis to identify optimal stopping points and prevent model degradation during the training process.

34.title: Towards a consistent interpretation of AIOps models
34.section: Research / Towards a consistent interpretation of AIOps models
34.description: Artificial Intelligence for IT Operations (AIOps) has been adopted in organizations in various tasks, including interpreting models to identify indicators of service failures. To avoid misleading practitioners, AIOps model interpretations should be consistent (i.e., different AIOps models on the same task agree with one another on feature importance). However, many AIOps studies violate established practices in the machine learning community when deriving interpretations.

35.title: Can I use this publicly available dataset to build commercial AI software?--A Case Study on Publicly Available Image Datasets
35.section: Research / Can I use this publicly available dataset to build commercial AI software?--A Case Study on Publicly Available Image Datasets
35.description: Publicly available datasets are one of the key drivers for commercial AI software. The use of publicly available datasets is governed by dataset licenses. These dataset licenses outline the rights one is entitled to on a given dataset and the obligations that one must fulfil to enjoy such rights without any license compliance violations. Unlike standardized Open Source Software (OSS) licenses, existing dataset licenses are defined in an ad-hoc manner and do not clearly outline the rights and obligations associated with their usage.

36.title: An experience report on producing verifiable builds for large-scale commercial systems
36.section: Research / An experience report on producing verifiable builds for large-scale commercial systems
36.description: We present an experience report on implementing verifiable builds for large-scale commercial software systems. Our report covers the challenges, solutions, and lessons learned from deploying verifiable build processes in industrial settings. The findings provide practical insights for organizations seeking to enhance their software supply chain security through build verification.

37.title: The Impact of Data Merging on the Interpretation of Cross-Project Just-In-Time Defect Models
37.section: Research / The Impact of Data Merging on the Interpretation of Cross-Project Just-In-Time Defect Models
37.description: Just-In-Time (JIT) defect models are classification models that identify the code commits that are likely to introduce defects. Cross-project JIT models have been introduced to address the suboptimal performance of JIT models when historical data is limited. However, many studies built cross-project JIT models using a pool of mixed data from multiple projects (i.e., data merging)—assuming that the properties of defect-introducing commits of a project are similar to that of the other projects, which is likely not true.

38.title: A survey of software log instrumentation
38.section: Research / A survey of software log instrumentation
38.description: We present a comprehensive survey of software log instrumentation practices, techniques, and tools. Our survey covers the evolution of logging frameworks, best practices for log instrumentation, and emerging trends in log analysis and management. The work provides guidance for developers and researchers on effective logging strategies for modern software systems.

39.title: SWE-Effi: Re-Evaluating SWE Agent Solutions for their Efficiency
39.section: Blog / SWE-Effi: Re-Evaluating SWE Agent Solutions for their Efficiency
39.description: Existing AI for software engineering leaderboards (e.g., SWE-bench ) focus solely on "resolve rate", ignoring the crucial factor of effectiveness in a resource-constrained world. This is a universal problem that also exists beyond software engineering: any AI system should be more than correct—it must also be cost-effective. We introduce SWE-Effi, a set of new metrics to re-evaluate AI systems in terms of holistic effectiveness scores. We define effectiveness as the balance between the resolve rate (the outcome) and the resources consumed (e.g., token and time). In this blog, we specifically focus on the software engineering scenario by re-ranking popular AI systems for issue resolution on a subset of the SWE-bench  benchmark using these new, multi-dimensional metrics. Note that by “AI system”, we refer to a single software system that includes an AI model (LLM) with a software scaffold (e.g., agent) working together to solve a given task.
